109 - Going Over Their Budgetary Process - Sales Training - YouTube.mp4
All right, today I'm gonna give you nine questions. These are NAPQ questions that you can ask to find out from your prospect what their real budget is. It is. Now, I'm going to show you an example of this in B2B, primarily. I'm also going to show you a few examples of B2C because it's a little bit different how you're going to ask that question, okay? I want you to remember, if you've been following me for a while or you're one of our clients in our virtual training courses or group training, the more pain the prospect experiences from your Questionability, okay, the bigger the gap in their mind, the more money the prospect will pay for your solution. They can only experience that pain by your questionability.
And how you're asking the questions, especially with your tone, that triggers them to go deep and brings them into their, what's called their emotional state. So I'm gonna give you some examples of any. Q budgeting questions, like I said, especially if you sold B2B, then I'm going to give you one or two if you sold B2C, because like I said, there are quite a few differences in how you're bringing it up, okay? First of all, go down to the bottom of this video, hit the subscribe button. That's probably important for you. Hit the subscribe button. And to the right of that, or maybe the left, I don't know, somewhere in there. There's like a little bell. That's your notifications button. Hit the notifications button as well.
So you get notified by YouTube every time I post a new training video, which I typically do two to four times a week. Here's one right here. Could you go over with me? Could you, John, could you go over with me your budgetary problem? Just so I have a better background. Mary, could you walk me through your budgetary process you have as an organization? Now, why would I ask that? That's a basic one, but it kind of sets up some other question. That allows you to understand how they're coming up with their budget to pay to solve the problems you've now helped them understand they have from your advanced questioning, okay? Here's another one. John, just to see if we could actually help you, but...
Can I understand the budgetary parameters that you're working with? Okay, that's a way to word that. Just to see if we could help you, what budgetary parameters are you actually... Working with so we have a better understanding of that now that question positions you as the expert who can help them solve their problems if they can get the funding together to do that if they can't get the funding then they don't get what they want they stay in the status quo and nothing ever changes all right I'm just those are basic ones there all right let's go through another one Mary can you tell me how the funding for this project will be determined? Or Barry can you tell me how the funding for this project is going to be determined?
Now why would you want to ask that? that. Like I said that's really only for B2B right there. You're not going to use that in a B2C situation. It wouldn't make any sense. But it's a question that helps them and you see how the funding process works in their company, because a lot of companies are different. And it's especially different if you're selling SMB compared if you're selling large enterprise like Fortune, you know, 100, 500, or 1,000 companies. You want to understand what other decision makers and influencers there are in that organization who you need to get them on board if you actually want to close that deal. More than likely, in 99% of the companies you're talking to, if you sell B2B, there's going to be more than one decision maker.
I think Forbes said the average company is about 6. 7 decision makers, all right? Here's another one. John, you had told me that your company... had set aside, I'm just gonna put in a number. You had told me your company had set aside $250,000 to fix XYZ problem. Can I ask how that was determined now? Why would I ask that question? This question really needs to be asked if you feel, from what they've said to you before, that the budget is low for your solution. So let me give you an example here. In this example, I said $250,000. So let's say they said to you in the beginning, 'Hey, we've got $250,000 to work with,' but after you went through your discovery process, you found out they had this problem, and that problem, and this problem, and that $250,000 is not going to solve all their problems.
They didn't realize the problems they had before. Questionability allowed them to start seeing it, you now realize that to really solve all the problems, it will be like $330,000 or $360,000, right? So now you... have to get them to think that maybe the budget they've been allocated is not going to be enough to really solve all their problems that you've helped them find they had and get them really where they want. So let me go over this again. John, you had told me that your company had set aside, and I don't even like to use the word budget, and here's why. Because when you get them into budget thinking, it gets them into like price or cost-based thinking rather than results-based thinking, okay?
So I like to sometimes say... You had told me that your company had set aside. It's just a different word than saying budget. It works better. You had told me that your company had set aside around $250,000 to fix XYZ problem. You repeat whatever that problem was. Can I ask how that was determined? Well, it was determined because of this and because of that and because of that. Then you're going to come here. Notice I crossed the word budget out. So do you feel like the funds you've been given is... Actually sufficient to actually solve these problems, though? See how I did that? See, I'm asking that in like a concern tone, okay? Do you feel like the... The funds you've been given by your CEO is...
I guess. sufficient to actually solve the problem though? Okay, now once again, you only say those two questions if they've told you the budget they have and you know through your questionability that you've taken... They are now being processed through now after 20 or 30 minutes, so we've got way more issues and problems that can be fixed, and that budget they originally told you about is going to be inadequate to actually get them where they want to go. Here's another one, okay? Now, this one right here is really good. In this situation, you use this in a B2B situation. If you have a first call discovery, you're talking with a C-level executive, you want to find out what obstacles they have in place of them to get this approved, okay?
Because if you don't, you're dead in the water, right? Mary, what obstacles would you possibly encounter when trying to get financial approval from the board to solve this problem? How about this one? Alex, can I ask... Like, I guess, what obstacles would you possibly encounter when trying to get financial approval to solve XYZ problems? Out. Well, you know, I next have to go to John and this department, and he's going to pass on. Well, tell me a little bit about John. Like, what are John's thoughts about the XYZ problems that you talked to me about? Well, he definitely knows they're there. Okay, but what happens when you go to John and he maybe doesn't want you to get the funding to put into XYZ? How are you going to get to A, B, and C?
We're asking consequence question. That's a whole other training, right? Here's a couple more I'm going to give you. So who else besides you would be involved in approving the budget to solve this? Can I ask who, besides you, would be involved in improving the budget to solve this? Or improving the funds? See, I don't like that 'budget' word there. How about 'funds', okay? Who else, besides you, would be involved in... the funding to solve this? Actually, I would say 'funds' if you sold B2B, okay? How will you get financial approval from the company to solve this issue you've talked about? Can I ask how? You're going to get financial approval from your company to solve XYZ problems. See how that works as well. Okay, those are good as well.
Here's another one: What type of funds do you have to put into this? So you can start, and this is more of a B2C situation, okay? So right here, this could be more B2C, business to consumer. And let's say that you kind of get some... red flags. They tell you that, you know, they're like, 'Is this going to cost me money?' Or I don't have a lot of money. How much do you think this is going to be or something? Whatever. You sell B2C. Well, I guess what type of funding do you have to put in so that you can, and then you repeat back what they said they wanted, okay? Like I said, this is more of a B2C type of sale, and you're going to add in that word 'funding' there, right?
Let's see what we got behind this screen. Maybe I'm going to give you more here. Yeah, we can do this. This could be more of a B2C option here as well. Let's say, like I said, they give you some red flags. They give you some red flags about... like, oh, is this going to cost me money? I don't have a lot of money. Things like that. Well, John, let's do this. First and foremost, I need to make sure that we could act. Help you. I'm assuming that you understand. When you start your own, let's say, for this example, I put here. Let's say that you sell some type of real estate training program. Okay. So you sell some type of real estate training program and it's going to cost like 15 grand or 20 grand.
And they invest in that and it's going to teach them how to scale the real estate where they can get 10, 15, 15, 20 properties a year, rather than now they've got a real estate business on the side and they're getting one property every two years, okay? So I'm assuming you understand. When you, you know, obviously when you're trying to scale your real estate company, that it's gonna require capital or credit to put into the right training. So that your business can actually, like you had said, get to at least 12 homes per year. Now with that in mind, okay, with your situation, what type of funding... Or credit do you have access to or can get access to to put in your business so you can scale to $10 million a year?
I'm just making up numbers. You're repeating back what... whatever they said. Okay. So there you go. There you have it. Those are some examples. Primarily the first like seven, eight questions are primarily going to be used B2B. You wouldn't use those at BNC. B2C sales at all. The last two examples I gave you are more of a B2C setting.
